8.1.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.1.title: Board Approval of Technology Risk Appetite
8.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the board actively oversees technology risk rather than merely receiving information passively. They look for evidence of board-level challenge, dedicated agenda time, approved budgets, and integration of technology risk into the enterprise risk management framework.

evidenceItems.name: Board meeting minutes discussing technology risk
evidenceItems.description: Board meeting minutes discussing technology risk
whatGoodLooksLike: Minutes show specific technology risk agenda items (not buried under AOB)
Evidence of board members asking questions and challenging management presentations
Clear action items with assigned owners and deadlines arising from technology risk discussions
Documented board decisions on technology risk matters such as budget approvals or risk acceptance
commonGaps: Technology risk mentioned only as a footnote or information item with no recorded discussion
No evidence of board members engaging, questioning, or challenging management
Missing action items or follow-up tracking from previous board technology risk discussions
Minutes are generic and do not reflect substantive deliberation on specific technology risks
suggestedSources: Board portal or collaboration platform (e.g., Diligent, BoardEffect)

evidenceItems.name: Enterprise risk management framework incorporating technology risk
evidenceItems.description: Enterprise risk management framework incorporating technology risk
whatGoodLooksLike: Technology risk is explicitly identified as a distinct risk category within the ERM framework
Clear linkages between technology risk and other risk categories such as operational, credit, and reputational risk
Defined risk taxonomy that includes technology-specific risk types such as cyber risk, data risk, and system availability risk
Framework references BNM RMiT requirements and maps controls to specific clauses
commonGaps: Technology risk treated as a subset of operational risk without distinct identification or assessment criteria
ERM framework last updated more than 12 months ago with no evidence of annual review
No mapping between technology risk categories and business impact scenarios
Framework does not reference or align with BNM RMiT policy requirements
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer, MetricStream)

evidenceItems.name: Board-approved technology risk budget and resource allocation
evidenceItems.description: Board-approved technology risk budget and resource allocation
whatGoodLooksLike: Dedicated line items for technology risk management in the annual budget, separate from general IT spending
Board resolution or minutes recording formal approval of the technology risk budget
Budget covers cybersecurity tools, risk personnel, training, third-party assessments, and incident response capabilities
Quarterly variance reports showing actual spend against approved budget presented to the board
commonGaps: Technology risk budget is bundled within general IT or operational budgets with no separate visibility
No formal board approval record for the technology risk budget allocation
Budget does not cover all critical areas such as incident response, training, or third-party risk assessments
No tracking of budget utilisation or variance reporting to the board

evidenceItems.name: Board terms of reference covering technology risk oversight
evidenceItems.description: Board terms of reference covering technology risk oversight
whatGoodLooksLike: Terms of reference explicitly include technology risk oversight as a board responsibility
Defined frequency and format for technology risk reporting to the board
Clear delineation of board versus management responsibilities for technology risk
Terms of reference reviewed and updated within the last 12 months
commonGaps: Terms of reference do not mention technology risk or cybersecurity oversight responsibilities
No defined reporting cadence or format for technology risk matters
Responsibilities for technology risk oversight are vague or conflated with general IT governance
Terms of reference have not been reviewed or updated in more than two years

auditTips: Request the last 4 quarters of board minutes and verify technology risk is a standing agenda item with dedicated discussion time, not buried under AOB
Cross-reference board-approved technology risk budget with actual spending reports to verify resource allocation matches stated commitments
Check whether the enterprise risk management framework explicitly identifies technology risk as a distinct category with its own assessment criteria
Verify that board terms of reference were reviewed within the last 12 months and explicitly cover technology risk oversight responsibilities
Look for evidence of board challenge by examining whether minutes record questions, disagreements, or requests for additional information from management

8.2.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.2.title: Board Oversight Responsibilities
8.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners verify that a dedicated board-level technology risk committee exists with qualified members, meets at least quarterly, and produces meaningful outcomes. They will scrutinise member competency profiles, attendance records, and whether the committee exercises genuine challenge rather than rubber-stamping management reports.

evidenceItems.name: Board committee charter or terms of reference
evidenceItems.description: Board committee charter or terms of reference
whatGoodLooksLike: Charter clearly defines the committee's mandate covering technology risk, cybersecurity, and IT resilience oversight
Specifies membership composition requirements including minimum technology or cybersecurity expertise
Defines quorum requirements, meeting frequency (at least quarterly), and reporting obligations to the full board
Includes authority to commission independent reviews and access any information necessary for its oversight role
commonGaps: Charter is generic and does not specifically address technology risk or cybersecurity oversight responsibilities
No minimum competency requirements for committee membership specified in the charter
Charter does not define the committee's authority to commission independent assessments or access information
Charter has not been reviewed or updated since initial approval

evidenceItems.name: Committee member profiles demonstrating relevant expertise
evidenceItems.description: Committee member profiles demonstrating relevant expertise
whatGoodLooksLike: Each member has a documented profile showing technology, cybersecurity, or IT risk management qualifications or experience
Profiles include relevant certifications (e.g., CISSP, CISM, CRISC), academic qualifications, or senior industry experience
Competency gaps are identified with documented plans for addressing them through training or advisory support
Profiles are updated annually or upon membership changes
commonGaps: Committee members lack any documented technology or cybersecurity expertise or experience
Profiles are outdated and do not reflect current competencies or recent training undertaken
No formal competency assessment performed against the requirements of the committee charter
Reliance on a single member for all technology expertise, creating a single point of failure

evidenceItems.name: Quarterly meeting schedules and attendance records
evidenceItems.description: Quarterly meeting schedules and attendance records
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual meeting schedule published at the start of each year with at least four meetings planned
Attendance records show consistent quorum achievement across all meetings
Individual member attendance rates are tracked and reported, with minimum attendance thresholds enforced
Ad hoc meetings are convened when material technology risk events occur outside the regular schedule
commonGaps: Fewer than four meetings held in the calendar year without documented justification
Repeated failure to achieve quorum, with meetings proceeding without sufficient attendees
No tracking of individual member attendance rates or consequences for poor attendance
No ad hoc meetings convened despite significant technology incidents occurring during the year

evidenceItems.name: Committee meeting minutes and action items
evidenceItems.description: Committee meeting minutes and action items
whatGoodLooksLike: Minutes reflect substantive discussion of technology risk topics with specific questions and challenges from committee members
Action items are clearly documented with assigned owners, deadlines, and priority levels
Follow-up on previous action items is a standing agenda item with status updates recorded
Decisions and recommendations to the full board are clearly documented and tracked through to board discussion
commonGaps: Minutes are brief summaries that do not capture the substance of discussions or member challenges
Action items lack assigned owners or deadlines, making follow-up impossible to track
No evidence of tracking or following up on action items from previous meetings
Committee recommendations to the board are not formally documented or tracked
suggestedSources: Board portal or document management system
Action item tracking system or register

auditTips: Request the committee charter and verify it explicitly mandates technology risk and cybersecurity oversight with quarterly meeting requirements
Review member profiles against the charter's competency requirements and check for documented plans to address any identified gaps
Count the actual number of meetings held in the past 12 months and verify quorum was achieved at each meeting
Examine the last 4 quarters of meeting minutes for evidence of genuine committee challenge, not just acknowledgement of management reports
Trace at least 3 action items from assignment through to completion or escalation to verify the action tracking process works

8.3.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.3.title: Board-Level Technology Committee
8.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will assess whether board members possess or have access to adequate technology and cybersecurity knowledge. They look for structured competency assessments, documented training records, evidence of external advisory engagement, and whether training content is relevant to current threats facing Malaysian financial institutions.

evidenceItems.name: Board training records on technology and cybersecurity topics
evidenceItems.description: Board training records on technology and cybersecurity topics
whatGoodLooksLike: Individual training records maintained for each board member showing dates, topics, duration, and provider
Training topics are current and relevant, covering areas such as cyber threats, cloud risk, data protection, and AI governance
At least two technology-focused training sessions conducted per year for the full board
Training materials are retained alongside attendance records for audit verification
Post-training feedback or knowledge assessments are documented
commonGaps: No formal training records exist; training is claimed but undocumented
Training topics are outdated or generic and do not address current threat landscape or Malaysian regulatory context
Training is conducted only once or irregularly rather than on a sustained annual programme
Some board members have no recorded technology training attendance at all

evidenceItems.name: External advisor engagement records
evidenceItems.description: External advisor engagement records
whatGoodLooksLike: Formal engagement letters or contracts with qualified technology or cybersecurity advisory firms
Advisor qualifications and credentials are documented and demonstrate relevant expertise
Records of advisory sessions, reports delivered, and recommendations made to the board
Evidence that advisory input influenced board decisions or risk appetite calibration
commonGaps: No external advisors engaged despite identified competency gaps in the board skills matrix
Advisor engagement is informal with no documented terms of reference or deliverables
Advisory recommendations are received but not tracked or acted upon by the board
Advisors lack relevant financial services or cybersecurity credentials
suggestedSources: External consulting firm reports and invoices

evidenceItems.name: Board competency assessment results
evidenceItems.description: Board competency assessment results
whatGoodLooksLike: Structured assessment covering technology domains such as cybersecurity, cloud computing, data governance, and digital banking risks
Individual results are documented with identified strengths and development areas for each board member
Assessment results are used to inform the annual board training plan and external advisory needs
Year-on-year comparison shows improvement in collective board technology competency
commonGaps: No formal competency assessment conducted; board technology knowledge is assumed rather than measured
Assessment is a generic self-evaluation with no objective measurement criteria
Results are not used to inform training plans or advisory engagement decisions
Assessment has not been updated to reflect new technology risk areas such as AI, open banking, or cloud adoption

evidenceItems.name: Annual training plan for board members
evidenceItems.description: Annual training plan for board members
whatGoodLooksLike: Training plan is approved at the start of each year with specific technology and cybersecurity topics scheduled
Plan is informed by the competency assessment results and addresses identified knowledge gaps
Plan includes a mix of internal briefings, external conferences, and specialist workshops
Completion tracking shows actual delivery against the planned schedule
commonGaps: No formal annual training plan exists; training is ad hoc and reactive
Plan exists on paper but actual delivery falls significantly short of planned sessions
Plan does not align with competency assessment findings or current threat landscape
Plan covers only generic governance topics without dedicated technology or cybersecurity content

auditTips: Request the board skills matrix and verify that technology and cybersecurity competency is explicitly assessed for each individual member
Cross-reference the training plan with actual training records to identify gaps between planned and delivered sessions
Verify that external advisors engaged have relevant qualifications and that their recommendations are traceable to board actions
Check whether training content is current and addresses threats specific to Malaysian financial services, not just generic cybersecurity awareness
Ask for evidence that competency assessment results directly informed the training plan and advisory engagement decisions

8.4.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.4.title: Board Discussion on Technology and Cyber Risk
8.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will assess whether the technology risk appetite statement contains both quantitative and qualitative measures, has current board approval, is reviewed at least annually, and is operationally embedded through cascaded limits and monitoring. They will test whether threshold breaches trigger defined escalation procedures.

evidenceItems.name: Board-approved technology risk appetite statement
evidenceItems.description: Board-approved technology risk appetite statement
whatGoodLooksLike: Statement explicitly covers cybersecurity, system availability, data integrity, and third-party technology risk domains
Includes both quantitative metrics (e.g., maximum 4 hours RTO for critical systems, fewer than 3 P1 incidents per quarter) and qualitative statements (e.g., zero tolerance for customer data breaches)
Carries formal board approval with documented resolution, signatures, and approval date
Version-controlled with clear effective date and next review date
Aligned with the institution's overall enterprise risk appetite statement
commonGaps: Statement contains only qualitative platitudes without measurable thresholds or metrics
No formal board approval record; statement exists as a management document without board endorsement
Statement has not been reviewed or updated in more than 12 months
Technology risk appetite is not aligned with or cross-referenced to the enterprise risk appetite statement
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer)

evidenceItems.name: Risk appetite metrics and thresholds
evidenceItems.description: Risk appetite metrics and thresholds
whatGoodLooksLike: Each metric has clearly defined green, amber, and red thresholds with specific numerical values
Metrics cover key domains including system uptime, incident frequency, vulnerability remediation timelines, and data loss events
Thresholds are calibrated based on historical performance data and industry benchmarks for Malaysian financial institutions
Metrics are mapped to specific risk appetite statements for traceability
commonGaps: Thresholds are arbitrary and not based on historical data, industry benchmarks, or business impact analysis
Metrics do not cover all technology risk domains referenced in the risk appetite statement
No differentiation between warning (amber) and breach (red) thresholds
Metrics have not been recalibrated despite significant changes in the institution's technology environment
suggestedSources: GRC or risk monitoring platform configuration
IT service management platform (e.g., ServiceNow ITSM)

evidenceItems.name: Annual review and approval records
evidenceItems.description: Annual review and approval records
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented review process showing what was assessed, what changed, and the rationale for changes or reaffirmation
Evidence that the review considered changes in the threat landscape, business strategy, regulatory requirements, and past incidents
Board meeting minutes or resolution recording formal re-approval of the reviewed risk appetite statement
Clear version history showing progression from previous to current approved version
commonGaps: No evidence of annual review despite the statement being more than 12 months old
Review is perfunctory with no documented analysis of whether thresholds remain appropriate
Review does not consider external factors such as emerging threats, regulatory changes, or industry incidents
Board re-approval is not formally documented in meeting minutes or resolutions

evidenceItems.name: Evidence of risk appetite communication to management
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of risk appetite communication to management
whatGoodLooksLike: Formal communication or memo from the CRO distributing the approved risk appetite statement to all relevant stakeholders
Acknowledgement records showing business unit heads and IT leaders have received and understood the risk appetite
Risk appetite is referenced in operational policies, procedures, and risk assessment templates
Training or briefing sessions conducted to explain risk appetite thresholds and their operational implications
commonGaps: Risk appetite statement resides only in board papers with no evidence of communication to operational teams
No formal acknowledgement process; communication assumed but not documented
Business units and IT teams are unaware of specific thresholds and cannot articulate the institution's technology risk appetite
Risk appetite is not referenced or embedded in operational risk management processes

auditTips: Verify the risk appetite statement contains both quantitative metrics with specific thresholds and qualitative statements, as BNM explicitly requires both
Check the approval date and confirm the statement has been reviewed within the last 12 months with board re-approval documented
Ask three different operational managers to articulate the institution's technology risk appetite to test whether communication has been effective
Request evidence of at least one threshold breach in the past year and trace the escalation path from detection through to board notification
Compare stated risk appetite metrics against actual performance data to identify any persistent breaches that have not been escalated or addressed

8.5.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.5.title: Board Audit Committee Technology Oversight
8.5.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the board receives comprehensive, quarterly technology risk reports that include KRIs, incident summaries, risk mitigation progress, trend analysis, and forward-looking assessments. They will check whether the board engages with the reports and whether resulting actions are documented and tracked.

evidenceItems.name: Quarterly technology risk reports to the board
evidenceItems.description: Quarterly technology risk reports to the board
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports are delivered quarterly without gaps or delays in the reporting cycle
Each report covers all mandatory elements: KRIs, incident summaries, mitigation status, trend analysis, and forward-looking assessments
Reports are written in business language accessible to board members, not technical jargon
Reports include executive summaries with clear risk ratings and recommended board actions
Trend analysis covers at least four consecutive quarters to show directional movement
commonGaps: Reports are missed or delivered late, with quarters without any board technology risk reporting
Reports cover only a subset of required elements, typically missing trend analysis or forward-looking assessments
Reports are overly technical and do not translate technology risks into business impact language
No executive summary or clear indication of what requires board attention or action
suggestedSources: Board portal or document management system
Chief Risk Officer reporting team records

evidenceItems.name: Key risk indicator dashboards
evidenceItems.description: Key risk indicator dashboards
whatGoodLooksLike: Dashboard includes clearly defined KRIs with green, amber, and red thresholds aligned to the risk appetite statement
KRIs cover all major technology risk domains: cybersecurity, availability, change management, and third-party risk
Historical trend lines are visible for each KRI spanning at least four quarters
Dashboard narrative explains the significance of any amber or red indicators and actions being taken
commonGaps: KRIs lack defined thresholds, making it impossible to determine whether the institution is within risk appetite
Dashboard shows only current-state indicators without historical trend data
Critical risk domains such as third-party technology risk or patch management are missing from the dashboard
No narrative or commentary accompanies the dashboard data, leaving the board to interpret raw numbers
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer, MetricStream)
SIEM or security monitoring platform dashboards

evidenceItems.name: Incident summary reports presented to board
evidenceItems.description: Incident summary reports presented to board
whatGoodLooksLike: Incidents are categorised by severity, type, root cause, and business impact
Summary includes both the number of incidents and their cumulative business impact (e.g., downtime hours, customers affected, financial loss)
Root cause analysis findings are summarised with systemic themes identified across incidents
Comparison with previous quarters shows whether incident frequency and severity are trending up or down
commonGaps: Only major incidents are reported; near misses and lower-severity incidents that reveal systemic issues are omitted
Incidents are counted but business impact is not quantified or communicated to the board
No root cause analysis themes are presented, missing the opportunity to highlight systemic weaknesses
Incident reporting is ad hoc rather than structured within the quarterly board report
suggestedSources: IT service management platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management)

evidenceItems.name: Risk mitigation status reports
evidenceItems.description: Risk mitigation status reports
whatGoodLooksLike: All open risk treatment plans are listed with current status, percentage completion, and expected closure dates
Overdue treatment plans are clearly highlighted with explanations and revised timelines
Reports show the number of risks closed versus new risks identified during the reporting period
Board is informed of any risks where the treatment approach has changed (e.g., from mitigate to accept)
commonGaps: Risk mitigation progress is not reported to the board, or only high-level summaries are provided without specifics
Overdue treatment plans are not flagged or explained to the board
No tracking of risk closure rates or comparison between new and resolved risks
Changes in risk treatment approach are made without board visibility or approval where required
suggestedSources: Risk management team tracking spreadsheets or registers
Project management office milestone reports for risk-related projects

evidenceItems.name: Board acknowledgement or sign-off records
evidenceItems.description: Board acknowledgement or sign-off records
whatGoodLooksLike: Board meeting minutes explicitly record that the technology risk report was presented, discussed, and acknowledged
Minutes capture specific questions, challenges, or concerns raised by board members regarding the report content
Resulting board directives or action items are documented with assigned owners and deadlines
Follow-up on previous board directives related to technology risk is recorded in subsequent meeting minutes
commonGaps: Minutes note the report was tabled but do not record any discussion or acknowledgement
No evidence of board members questioning or challenging the reported information
Board directives arising from technology risk discussions are not documented as formal action items
Follow-up on previous board directives is not tracked or reported in subsequent meetings
suggestedSources: Board portal action item tracking module

auditTips: Request the last 4 quarterly board reports and verify each contains all mandatory elements: KRIs, incident summaries, mitigation status, trend analysis, and forward-looking assessments
Check that KRI thresholds in the dashboard align with those defined in the board-approved risk appetite statement
Verify that incident summaries include business impact quantification, not just incident counts
Trace at least 2 board directives from technology risk report discussions through to completion or ongoing tracking
Confirm no quarters were missed in the reporting cycle and that reports were delivered to the board in advance of meetings

8.6.title: Senior Management Accountability for Technology Risk
8.6.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that senior management accountability for technology risk is formally documented, that a CISO or equivalent is formally appointed with direct board or committee reporting access, and that this reporting line is actively exercised. They will scrutinise the CISO's organisational placement and independence from IT operations.

evidenceItems.name: Senior management accountability matrix for technology risk
evidenceItems.description: Senior management accountability matrix for technology risk
whatGoodLooksLike: Matrix assigns specific technology risk domains to named C-suite executives (e.g., CISO for cybersecurity, CTO for infrastructure, CRO for risk oversight)
Each accountability area has clearly defined responsibilities, decision authority limits, and escalation triggers
Matrix distinguishes between first line (risk ownership), second line (risk oversight), and third line (audit) responsibilities
Matrix is board-approved and reviewed annually or upon significant organisational changes
commonGaps: No formal accountability matrix exists; technology risk responsibilities are assumed based on job titles rather than documented
Accountability overlaps between roles without clear delineation of who is ultimately responsible for specific risk domains
Matrix does not reflect the three lines of defence model expected by BNM
Matrix has not been updated following organisational restructuring or leadership changes

evidenceItems.name: CISO appointment letter and job description
evidenceItems.description: CISO appointment letter and job description
whatGoodLooksLike: Formal appointment letter signed by the CEO or board chairman confirming the CISO designation
Job description explicitly covers cybersecurity strategy, incident response leadership, security architecture governance, and regulatory compliance
Mandate includes authority to halt or delay technology deployments that present unacceptable security risks
Role grade and reporting structure demonstrate appropriate seniority within the organisation
commonGaps: No formal appointment letter; the CISO role is assumed rather than formally designated
Job description is generic IT management rather than specific to information security leadership
CISO lacks documented authority to influence technology decisions on security grounds
Role is positioned too junior in the organisation to have the influence required by RMiT
suggestedSources: Human Resources appointment and contract records

evidenceItems.name: CISO reporting line documentation
evidenceItems.description: CISO reporting line documentation
whatGoodLooksLike: Organisation chart clearly shows the CISO with a direct reporting line to the board or board technology risk committee for cybersecurity matters
Dual reporting structure is documented if the CISO has both an administrative reporting line (e.g., to CEO) and a functional reporting line to the board committee
Reporting line demonstrates independence from the CTO or Head of IT operations
Documentation is current and reflects the actual organisational structure in practice
commonGaps: CISO reports exclusively to the CTO or Head of IT with no independent board access
Organisation chart shows a dotted line to the board but no evidence that this reporting line is exercised
Reporting structure has the CISO buried several layers below the C-suite, limiting influence and access
Organisation chart is outdated and does not reflect current reporting arrangements

evidenceItems.name: Board or committee meeting records showing CISO reporting
evidenceItems.description: Board or committee meeting records showing CISO reporting
whatGoodLooksLike: Minutes show the CISO presenting directly to the board or committee at least quarterly
CISO presentations cover cybersecurity posture, incident trends, threat intelligence, and security programme progress
Evidence of board members engaging with the CISO directly, asking questions, and requesting follow-up information
CISO has the opportunity to raise concerns directly to the board without management filtering
commonGaps: No records of the CISO ever presenting directly to the board or committee despite having a documented reporting line
CISO presentations are delivered through the CTO or CRO rather than directly, undermining independence
CISO attendance at board or committee meetings is irregular with multiple quarters without direct reporting
Minutes do not show the CISO being given the opportunity to raise independent concerns
suggestedSources: Board portal presentation and document records

auditTips: Verify the CISO appointment is formally documented with a board-approved mandate and confirm the appointee has appropriate seniority and qualifications
Examine the organisation chart and confirm the CISO has an independent reporting line to the board or committee that does not pass through the CTO
Request the last 4 quarters of committee minutes and verify the CISO presented directly at each meeting, not through an intermediary
Check that the senior management accountability matrix reflects the three lines of defence model with clear boundaries between risk ownership and risk oversight
Ask the CISO whether they have the authority to delay technology deployments on security grounds and request evidence of this authority being exercised

8.7.title: Cross-Functional Technology Committee
8.7.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that an independent technology risk management function exists with adequate authority, resources, and access. They will scrutinise organisational independence from IT operations, assess staffing adequacy relative to the institution's technology complexity, and review whether the function actively produces risk assessments and reports.

evidenceItems.name: Technology risk management function charter
evidenceItems.description: Technology risk management function charter
whatGoodLooksLike: Charter clearly defines the function's mandate covering risk identification, assessment, monitoring, and reporting across all technology domains
Authority to access any technology system, data, or personnel necessary for risk assessment is explicitly stated
Independence from IT operations is explicitly mandated in the charter with board or committee approval
Charter specifies reporting obligations to the CRO, senior management, and board committee
Document is version-controlled with board approval date and next review date
commonGaps: No formal charter exists; the function operates based on informal understanding rather than documented authority
Charter does not explicitly mandate independence from IT operations or define access authority
Charter is outdated and does not reflect current scope of technology risk across cloud, digital, and third-party domains
Charter lacks board or committee approval, undermining the function's formal authority

evidenceItems.name: Organisation chart showing independence from IT operations
evidenceItems.description: Organisation chart showing independence from IT operations
whatGoodLooksLike: Organisation chart clearly shows the technology risk function reporting to the CRO or a risk leadership role separate from the CTO or Head of IT
No dotted or solid reporting line connects the function to IT operations leadership
Function head has an appropriate title and grade reflecting the seniority needed to exercise authority across the institution
Chart is current, dated, and reflects the actual organisational structure
commonGaps: Technology risk function reports to the CTO or Head of IT, creating a conflict of interest
Organisation chart is outdated and does not reflect recent restructuring or leadership changes
Function is embedded within the IT department with no structural independence
Function head is too junior in the organisation to effectively challenge IT operations leadership

evidenceItems.name: Staffing plan and competency matrix
evidenceItems.description: Staffing plan and competency matrix
whatGoodLooksLike: Staffing plan is proportionate to the institution's technology complexity and maps roles to the function's mandate
Competency matrix defines required skills across risk management, cybersecurity, cloud, data, and technology audit disciplines
Current staff competencies are assessed against the matrix with documented gaps and development plans
Staffing plan includes succession planning for critical roles within the function
Staff hold relevant certifications such as CRISC, CISM, CISSP, or ISO 27001 Lead Auditor
commonGaps: Function is staffed with only one or two individuals for an institution with a complex technology environment
No competency matrix exists; staffing is based on availability rather than required competencies
Identified competency gaps have no documented development plans or hiring timelines
Staff lack relevant risk management or technology certifications
suggestedSources: Head of Technology Risk personnel files and development plans
Professional development and certification tracking records

evidenceItems.name: Function mandate and authority documentation
evidenceItems.description: Function mandate and authority documentation
whatGoodLooksLike: Documentation confirms the function's authority to conduct risk assessments across all technology domains without restriction
Evidence of the function exercising its authority through completed risk assessments, audit findings, and risk advisories
Access rights to technology systems, SIEM, vulnerability scanners, and CMDB are documented and verified
Evidence of the function providing independent risk opinions on major technology projects or changes
commonGaps: Mandate exists on paper but there is no evidence of the function exercising its authority in practice
Function has been denied access to systems or information needed for risk assessments
No evidence of the function providing independent risk opinions on technology projects or changes
Function's authority has been overridden by IT operations leadership without proper escalation
suggestedSources: Technology risk assessment reports and work products
System access logs and permissions records
Risk advisory memos and project risk sign-off records

auditTips: Verify organisational independence by examining the actual reporting line of the technology risk function head and confirming it does not report to IT operations
Assess staffing adequacy by comparing the number of risk staff against the institution's technology complexity (number of critical systems, cloud deployments, third-party integrations)
Request examples of risk assessments completed by the function in the past 12 months to verify it is actively producing work products
Ask the function head whether they have ever been denied access to systems or information and how such situations were resolved
Review the function's output (risk reports, advisories, assessment findings) to determine whether it provides genuine independent challenge to IT operations
